title: Sensitivity Evaluation of SU(1,1) Interferometers with Arbitrary Input Probe State and Homodyne Detections
authors: Sonu Jana, Dhruv Baheti, Paul Grossiord, Fabien Bretenaker, Nadia Belabas, Syamsundar De
abstract: We provide a general theoretical derivation of the phase sensitivity achieved by SU(1,1) interferometers under homodyne detection. The general expressions obtained accommodate arbitrary input states and include internal and external losses. In this systematic review, both full SU(1,1) interferometers with two parametric amplifiers and the truncated interferometers with only one parametric amplifier are examined. We investigate scenarios involving both single-output ports and joint homodyne detection, and consider parametric amplifiers with equal gains or with a boosted gain second amplifier. Our analytical formulation provides physical insight and understanding of the improvements in the sensitivity, which are shown to originate from noise reduction and/or signal amplification, depending on the configurations and practical implementations. Surprisingly, the configuration with single-output mode detection and parametric amplifiers with equal gains exhibits the highest robustness to very high internal losses. We finally apply this framework to a ubiquitous $|α,0\rangle$ input two-mode coherent probe state. This approach permits the comparison of different strategies and the optimization of the interferometer performance in the presence of losses. In particular, we determine which amplification and detection configurations provide the best performance, depending on the level of losses. This exemplifies how this general analytical approach provides a powerful tool to design quantum-enhanced interferometers and achieve optimal sensitivity with selected probe states and homodyne detection.
summary: This paper establishes a unified theoretical framework to predict the phase sensitivity of SU(1,1) interferometers for any arbitrary input state. It identifies how different gain and detection configurations can be optimized to mitigate the degrading effects of internal and external optical losses. The findings provide a roadmap for achieving optimal quantum-enhanced sensing in practical, noisy environments.
main_problem: Developing a generalized theoretical framework to evaluate the phase sensitivity of SU(1,1) interferometers using arbitrary two-mode input states and various homodyne detection schemes under the influence of internal and external losses.
main_result: The study provides a comprehensive analytical formulation showing that dark-fringe operation is more robust to external losses and identifies that single-port detection with balanced gains is highly robust to internal losses. It also proves that for certain inputs, the full SU(1,1) interferometer is equivalent to the truncated version under joint homodyne detection.
method: The authors use a Heisenberg representation and Bogoliubov-type transformations to propagate field operators through the interferometer, deriving analytical expressions for quadrature variances and signal derivatives.
model_or_system: The system consists of SU(1,1) interferometers (both full two-amplifier and truncated single-amplifier configurations) utilizing optical parametric amplifiers (PAs) and subject to models of internal and external optical losses.
key_observables: Field quadrature operators (X and Y), single-port homodyne detection outputs, and joint homodyne detection outputs (sum and difference of photocurrents).
important_parameters: Parametric amplifier gains (G, g), phase shift (phi), internal loss coefficients (L), external loss coefficients (l), and input state statistics (e.g., coherent state amplitude alpha).
main_assumptions: Lossy modes are assumed to be initialized in the vacuum state; the analysis focuses specifically on homodyne detection rather than photon-counting.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the schematics of full, truncated, and lossy SU(1,1) interferometers and different detection schemes. Figure 2 compares phase sensitivity across different phase biases. Figure 5 provides a color-coded map of optimal configurations based on loss levels. Figure 6 illustrates sensitivity variation as a function of external losses for different detection ports.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a general derivation of interferometer transformations, followed by the evolution of field quadratures and statistical moments. It then evaluates sensitivity at bright and dark fringes, compares different gain regimes and detection strategies, and concludes with a specific application to coherent state inputs and a summary of optimal configurations under loss.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it provides a rigorous tool for designing loss-resilient quantum-enhanced sensors and understanding the interplay between amplification, squeezing, and decoherence.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) Schematic of a full SU(1,1) interfer- ometer. The beam splitters of a conventional Mach- Zehnder interferometer are replaced by parametric am- plifiers (PAs). A strong pump drives the first PA, which mixes the two modes (signal (s) and idler (i)) of an arbitrary two-mode input state ρs,i. After the first PA (characterized by g1 and G1 see main text), a phase shift ϕ = ϕ0 + δϕ is applied to one mode (signal) relative to the pump and the other mode (idler). Both modes, to- gether with the pump, then act as inputs to the second PA (characterized by g2 and G2). Internal and external losses are modeled by fictitious beam splitters placed in- side and outside the interferometer...

Figure 2: Phase sensitivity as a function of the phase shift bias ϕ0 = ϕ −δϕ. The phase of the coherent in- put with respect to the external reference is taken to be zero. The plots correspond to balanced and joint homo- dyne configuration with PA gains G1 = G2 = 2 and an unbalanced single-port homodyne case with G1 = 2 and G2 = 5. The vertical axis is calibrated with respect to the phase shift sensitivity of a Mach-Zehnder interfer- ometer, which achieves ∆ϕ|α| = 1

Figure 3: Phase sensitivity as a function of asymmetric internal loss for the three detection schemes. Obtained from Eqs. 17 and 22. Gain values used for these plots: balanced gain configuration: G = 2; unbalanced gain configuration: G1 = 2, G2 = 5; joint homodyne de- tection: G = 2. Together, these plots illustrate the asymmetric impact of losses in the two arms. (a) The losses Ls in the signal arm are varied while the losses in the other arm are kept fixed (Li = 0.2). The dotted blue horizontal line corresponds to the phase shift sensi- tivity of a Mach-Zehnder interferometer which achieves ∆ϕ|α| = 1 (b). The losses Li in the idler arm are varied while the losses in the other arm remain...

Figure 4: Contour plot of the phase sensitivity versus losses in both arms of the SU(1,1) interferometer for (a) single-port detection with balanced gains (G = 2), (b) single-port detection with unbalanced gains (G1 = 2, G2 = 5), (c) joint homodyne detection with balanced gains (G = 2).

Figure 5: Choice of the gain and detection configu- rations leading to the best sensitivity for varying val- ues of the losses Ls and Li. In each region, the displayed color corresponds to the best configuration. Green: single-port detection with balanced gains (G = 2); Blue: single-port detection with unbalanced gains (G1 = 2, G2 = 5); Red: joint homodyne detection with balanced gains (G = 2). The color becomes lighter when the sensitivity improves. This plot is based on the data of Fig. 4. All values are normalized to the case of the Mach-Zehnder interferometer probed with the same in- put state. Black boxes around color bars show the range of values used in the plot.
